title: Managing chickens in summer heat
subtitle: Shade, ventilation, water, and what to do when a bird overheats
excerpt: Chickens are more sensitive to heat than to cold. A bird in serious heat stress can die within hours. This guide covers the practical steps to keep your flock cool in summer, how to read the signs of heat stress, and the immediate response if a bird is in trouble.
sourceNotes: Gail Damerow, The Chicken Health Handbook, 2nd ed. (2015), chapter on environmental hazards. University of Kentucky College of Agriculture, Cooperative Extension Service, Poultry Heat Stress fact sheet. RSPCA, Caring for chickens in hot weather (guidance note).

glossaryTerms.definition: A condition in which a bird's body temperature rises above the safe range because it cannot lose heat fast enough. Chickens regulate temperature through panting and blood flow to the comb and wattles. When these mechanisms cannot keep up, organ damage and death follow quickly.

Rapid open-beak breathing in chickens used to lose heat through evaporation from the respiratory tract. It is the main heat-loss mechanism available to birds, which cannot sweat. Prolonged heavy panting causes carbon dioxide loss and respiratory alkalosis.

content.text: Chickens have a thermal comfort zone of roughly 18 to 24 degrees Celsius. Above 27 degrees they start to show
behaviours. Above 35 degrees a heavy breed bird can die within a few hours. Silkies, heavy breeds, and birds carrying extra condition are most at risk.

with the beak open is the first visible sign. A bird in mild heat stress will also hold her wings slightly away from her body to allow air circulation and seek shade. A bird in serious heat stress will be unable to stand, may have a blue or purple comb, and will look floppy and unresponsive. This is an emergency.
Mild: open-beak panting, wings slightly spread, seeking shade and cool ground.
Moderate: panting heavily, lethargy, not moving much, pale face.
Severe: cannot stand, blue or dark comb, floppy neck, unresponsive. Act immediately.

Shade is the most important factor. A closed, poorly ventilated coop in direct sun in summer is hotter than outside. Prop the coop door open all day. If the run is fully exposed, rig a tarpaulin or shade cloth over part of it so birds can choose shade without going back inside the coop. Plant fast-growing annual climbers along the south side of a run to add natural shade by July.

Open all vents fully in summer. A coop at night in July can reach 30 degrees even after dark if ventilation is blocked. The overnight temperature inside should be close to the outside temperature. If the coop has only one vent, add a second on the opposite wall to create a cross-draught. Remove solid panels from pop-holes during the hottest weeks and replace them with wire mesh.

Water consumption doubles or triples in hot weather. Check drinkers at midday as well as morning and evening. Place drinkers in shade so they stay cool. Warm water is less effective for cooling and birds may drink less of it. Add a second water point on the far side of the run so birds at the bottom of the pecking order are not blocked from drinking. On very hot days, drop ice cubes into the drinker.

attrs.title: Feed timing in a heatwave
attrs.body: Digesting food generates heat. In a heatwave, birds eat less and this is normal. Do not try to increase food intake during the hottest days. Move the main feed session to early morning and again in the evening when the temperature drops. Avoid corn and high-energy snacks in the afternoon as these raise body temperature during digestion.

content.text: What to do if a bird overheats

Move the bird immediately to a cool, shaded space. Wet her legs and feet in cool (not cold) water. Do not use ice water directly on the body; a sharp temperature drop can cause circulatory shock. Wet the comb and wattles gently. Offer water but do not force it. Set up a fan in the recovery space if available. A bird that recovers enough to stand and drink on her own within 20 minutes usually survives. A bird that does not respond in that time needs a vet.

Wet an area of bare ground in the run in the morning so birds can stand on damp cool soil. Hang bunches of fresh greens in the shade to encourage activity in the cool part of the run. Avoid handling birds on hot days. Body heat from your hands adds to their heat load. Plan any routine checks for early morning.

entries.cue: Birds refusing to leave the coop even when the door is open
entries.cause: Run has no shade and the coop, although hot, feels safer than exposed ground in full sun
entries.fix: Create shade in the run before opening the coop. Even a piece of plywood propped at an angle provides enough shadow for birds to sit under.

entries.cue: Egg production stops during a heatwave
entries.cause: Normal stress response; energy goes to temperature regulation rather than production
entries.fix: Ensure water and shade are adequate. Egg production usually resumes within two to three days of cooler weather. No action needed if the birds look well otherwise.

entries.cue: Drinker empties too fast and birds seem to be wasting water
entries.cause: Birds are splashing and sitting in the drinker rather than just drinking
entries.fix: Add a shallow pan of water on the ground separately for birds to stand in. This gives them the cooling they are seeking without depleting the drinking supply.
